phenomenon: proximity-based approach behavior toward valued stimuli
claim: Spontaneous motor-execution patterns reveal selective approach preferences that correlate with personal traits and value systems.

phenomenon: spontaneous affective response to high-salience cues
claim: Frequent users develop automatic positive associations with category-specific cues that drive behavioral persistence.

phenomenon: reluctance to engage in directed mental simulation
claim: People systematically resist certain types of prompted imagining, with genre classification influencing engagement.

phenomenon: attribution of intentionality to behavior
claim: Folk judgments of whether behavior counts as intentional follow systematic linguistic patterns.

phenomenon: sex-typed task allocation in partnership labor
claim: Husbands' participation in female-typed domestic work follows a universal ordering principle that masks underlying patterns in cross-national analysis.

phenomenon: collective meaning-making toward supranational institution during crisis
claim: Economic disruption and institutional intervention reshape how citizens frame abstract collective membership across multiple interpretive dimensions.

phenomenon: transmission of parental advantage to timing of partnership entry
claim: Parental status delays youth union formation across countries, but effect strength tracks modernization level; education partially mediates.

phenomenon: age-related shifts in allocation of personal resources
claim: Older adults show heightened discrimination based on social proximity, favoring kin over non-kin and allocating differently by resource type.

phenomenon: behavioral change and capability development in chronic self-care
claim: Structured training in forward-planning and self-regulation increases confidence, goal achievement, and action initiation.

phenomenon: unified perception of replicated identical objects
claim: Viewing identical units grouped together increases perceived goal-coherence and functional effectiveness relative to single-unit perception.

phenomenon: time allocation across activity domains during competing commitment
claim: Unresolved goal tension shifts the person toward easier-to-justify activities and away from harder-to-justify ones, even when unrelated to the conflict.

phenomenon: spending preference under resource scarcity
claim: Perceived financial limits increase preference for durable goods over temporary experiences due to concern about lasting value.

phenomenon: parental investment allocation to offspring by sex
claim: Poor economic conditions increase allocation to daughters because perceived mate-scarcity reduces boys' reproductive prospects.

phenomenon: geometric form influence on product attribute judgment
claim: Circular/angular logo shapes activate softness/hardness associations that propagate to product attribute inferences via mental imagery.

phenomenon: cognitive flexibility under perceived limitation
claim: Salient resource scarcity reduces entrenched thinking about object use and increases novelty in problem-solving.

phenomenon: lateral positioning influence on preference and choice
claim: Placing healthy options leftward aligns with mental categories, easing processing and strengthening restraint.

phenomenon: activation of competitive threat perception and aggressive physiology
claim: Limited-availability marketing activates perception of other people as obstacles, triggering physiological aggression response.

phenomenon: color properties influence spatial perception
claim: Saturated color captures attention and heightens arousal, causing downstream inflation in size judgments and willingness-to-pay.

phenomenon: preference for divisive goods as identity markers
claim: Products with bimodal rating distributions signal distinctiveness and personality, appealing to people seeking individual differentiation.

phenomenon: subjective assessment of personal financial security
claim: Two separable dimensions of financial well-being (current stress and future security) jointly predict overall life satisfaction and track life domain importance.
